speaker: interviewer
text: Survivor is Gerda, [? Marcus ?] [? Wilchfort. ?] The date is December 17, 1995. Gerda, we left off, on the last tape, with your trip, now, to Switzerland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Like I said, we-- we were sitting at breakfast in that little cafe in France, in a small town. And halfways between Belgium and Switzerland, the Swiss border. And they came in and-- the-- the SS came in and looked at everybody, and didn't think that we were Jews, and-- and left again. And we were absolutely traumatized. That was one of the worst moments on that trip because there, our hearts stood still. We didn't dare breathing. And luckily, the children, there was two-- two of them, were quite small, didn't say anything either. And it went by. And I forgot to mention that we had a I suppose, Belgian man with us on the trip that was supposed to-- to direct us and chaperone us, but did not sit with us. But it-- once in a while, he made communication with us. But he watched over us, so to speak. And on the next leg of the trip, he came to us. And he said he's sorry, but that's as far as he can go. Because the border itself, close to the border, 100 miles from the border, or 50 miles from the border, the supervision of the Germans is very tight. And they have security all over, and he cannot jeopardize his organizations if he is being caught. So we are on our own now. And he told us what to do. And he said, when you come to this border village, you ask for Gerhard. Gerhard is the one who will take you over the border. He's part of this organization, and he knows about you. And ask around for Gerhard. Everybody will know who, where, he is.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were a little bit afraid. We went close to the border. But then to this village, there was no train anymore. There was only a bus. So we thought it would be wiser if we separate. And we put the lady with the small children on the bus. And mother and I, we walked.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had just a little suitcase. We were only-- we only had one spare set of underwear and stockings between us. And when one was washing, the other one was wearing, you know. And very little, we had-- just had a little-- a little satchel in our hands. And it was summer. And we were walking.

speaker: interviewer
text: So here comes the French gendarme on a bicycle. And he stops us and asks us for papers. So my mother showed him there, these French papers.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he didn't believe it. He knew who we were. And he gave us a whole interrogation. And then finally, my mother said, well, listen, you are French. And you are against the Germans. Oh, yes, Madame, he said.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, that's true, he said. So what do you want from us? Even if we are Jews, you are against the Germans more than against the Jews. So leave us alone. And he said, you're right. And he let us go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we came to this village, and the bus was already there. And these people on look came out. And but this whole village was full of Germans and SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we could not-- I think we stayed maybe one night. But it was really not safe to stay there. And I went around this village and I asked everybody for Gerhard? Gerhard?

speaker: interviewee
text: And everybody clammed up. Nobody wanted to know anything about Gerhard. Till finally, we heard from somebody that he was arrested, that he has been caught transporting people over the border, and that he was not there anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: So here, we started walking again. We walked up the highway on a very nice French landscape, up and down. And we came to a little country inn.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went in there, and we asked if they have two rooms, you know, one room for them, and one. So they said yes. We can stay there. And we didn't say anything. We had some French money, you know. Like I said, this woman had money. And we stayed at that inn. And we helped them with the cooking a little, and we ate there. And we didn't say anything. But somehow, they understood. The people understood. And it was very close to the Swiss border. And my mother said, if they want to get rid of us, they will help us do something, you know. They cannot keep us here forever. And if they know we're Jews, it's dangerous for them, you know, to hide us and to give us shelter. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: So sure enough, one day-- we stayed there almost a week, I think, till something happened. And I think I went back with the-- with the oldest boy. I went back one day to this village and asked again for Gerhard. And I think he-- well, you know, and we couldn't do anything. We couldn't find a way out. So she must have talked to the priest of the village, the owner of the inn. And then the next she said, tomorrow we'll have a hay wagon come in. And we'll put you into nice aprons.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you're going to sit on the hay wagon among the milk, big milk containers. And you can hide your little belongings under the hay. And a letter carrier on a bicycle will patrol the road, will see if there's Germans on the road. And we know when the Germans go for dinner. And we'll try to get you through to the border while they are at dinner. But the letter carrier will go up and down the road and watch and give the-- the driver of this hay wagon signals.

speaker: interviewer
text: So with our heart in our mouths, we went into this hay wagon. And the children were sitting in the back. And sure enough, this letter carrier was up and down, up and down.

speaker: interviewee
text: He came, you know, and watched for us, watched the road. And we had to go back to this village. The road went back to this village, and then another road to the thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we made it. And it was-- there was a farm. And I will never forget. It was Machpelah, like the cave, Machpelah, that the farm's name was [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a wooden table. And this farmer gave us all a cup of milk. And we sat at this table. And he said, go straight down. There was a creek. There's some barbed wire. Be careful. But you have to climb down this embankment. And there's a barbed wire is the border. Well, we went down. And that little girl had a pacifier. And she lost that pacifier. And she was only about two or three years old, three and a half. I don't know. And she screamed bloody murder. She must have been scared too, you know, after all this, [INAUDIBLE]. So she screamed bloody murder. And we-- but we made it over the barbed wire, or under the barbed wire, and made it into Switzerland. And those other ladies, they wanted to hug and kiss, and we're free. And my mother says, wait. Wait. Let's go. Let's go. Don't. We're not there yet. And we went up the other side the hill. And sure enough, the Swiss border guard had heard her and had-- and came running. But and at that time, they let refugees in, especially with children. But about four or six weeks before that, they sent a lot of refugees back. Because they didn't want to be in the bad graces with the Germans. And they had sent a lot of refugees back into France. And a lot of people perished that way. But we didn't know all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he took us and-- and-- and helped the children. And we walked again. We walked to the next little town.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was Chaux-de-Fonds. And he put-- they put us in a hotel, or put us up in a hotel. We told them that we had relatives in Zurich. I said before that we had-- they were-- as a matter of fact, with the same name. It was Blachmann. And it was some relative of my father. I don't know. And they said, yeah, they were-- they're going to call them, and put us in a hotel. Everything was fine. My mother goes in the bathroom. She comes out. She says, you know. We're not free. The policemen is standing there. He watches where we go and where I sleep. And he's going to be with us all the time. And he guarded us. And the next day, he drove us-- oh, it was a bus or something, I don't remember-- to the police station. And we had-- we were all day long at the police station, you know, cross-examining us and everything. And of course, we told them that these are false papers, that our names are so and so, the right names and everything. And they took everything down, and they gave us lunch. And they were very nice. And then they said, all right. You go with this policeman now. And you go wherever he will take you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to Neuchatel. I think on the train. It was very close by. But that was a bigger town.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he marched us to the town. And the-- the people from the-- they looked out of the windows, you know, in the little Swiss town.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they said, oh, they are-- they're poor people. They're poor people. They're-- they're going to jail. My mother says, to jail?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, sure enough, he took us to jail. And they separated the boy from us, from the women. And before we knew it, we were in jail. And an old, old jail with keys like this, big, like, keys. And they put my mother and me in one cell with one bed. And we listened. And all of a sudden, we heard Hebrew songs being sung, and Jewish songs being sung.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were other refugees in this jail. And they wanted us to know that they are there, and we are not alone. And it's going to be all right. So--

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRIES] We-- we made do. We had one narrow bed, so one slept this way, and one step this way. So we were, you know. to have more room. And we had all kinds of adventures in this old jail. The next morning, we-- there was a big keyhole. All of a sudden, I look, and I look. It comes, a pat of butter through this keyhole on a-- on a fork. I said, look, Mom, they-- there's something there. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened, there was a-- a Swiss--

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --convict. But he was not convicted yet. He was waiting for-- well, he was-- was a criminal. But white-collar criminal, I think. And he had money. And he got breakfast sent in every day. He had all his meals sent in every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he must have looked through the keyhole and seen us there. So he wanted to communicate with us or something. I don't know. So we had all kinds of things happening in this jail. And we were there about six weeks. Why-- why did they imprison you? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: There came a big influx of-- of refugees. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And I guess they didn't know what to do with them. I don't know. So-- and the camps, well, now, they had camps. But I guess they had to-- to process them, or who knows with the Swiss. They're not too friendly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you treated well in the jail, though?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. Yeah. So then came the High Holidays. So the Jewish families from Chaux-de-Fonds, from another town, from a bigger town, invited us to go to come for Rosh Shoshana to-- to-- to their synagogue. So they took us all by a train over there. But the men, again, they put to jail. The women, they paid for the hotel. But for the men, the few men that there were, was already not enough money. And we went there, to the synagogue. And we had a meal in the hotel there. And the men came to the hotel. And then the police took them back to the jail. And Yom Kippur, they said for fasting, you don't have to come. You can fast in the-- in jail. So we did. Then one of the men had a little siddur, and he-- he prayed. He did all the prayers. And my mother asked the prison guard, please keep the meal for us. We will eat tomorrow. Or bring it, if you can keep it for us, for the evening meal. We'll eat it in the evening. And we did. We fasted, and they left us. And they left the doors open. When the other prisoners were back in their cells, we had kind of a free reign on that floor. There was a big hallway and a window, on the-- on the [? edge. ?] And we could mingle with the other refugees, but only on that floor. And once a week, they took us to the basement for a bath to wash our hair, and to-- So-- And then slowly, most of the refugees, well, all of the refugees were taken out of the jails and taken to other camps. And we were, mother and I were mostly left alone. So in the meantime, a family that had a nice department store in that Neuchatel, they came, always, to see us and to, you know, to see what we need or if we can do something.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the-- the guard was really-- there was a couple, it was an older couple. And they were very nice. And he said to those people, if you guarantee that this Mrs. Blachmann and her daughter will come back again, I can let them go to see you once [? and ?] [? anything. ?] So a very strange situation started. We went. They opened the jail doors, and we walked in town alone. And we went to this lady.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was a very gracious lady. She made a beautiful tea for us with patisserie and with everything. And we were human beings all of a sudden, again. And when this was done, we marched back to jail. And we stayed in jail. I mean, it was really strange.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then winter started. And they said, do you have anything to wear? And we said, no.

speaker: interviewee
text: We don't have any. So we say, come to the store and pick out whatever you like, you know, what you need, underwear and stockings. Swiss winters are pretty severe.

speaker: interviewer
text: So and then we had a little bit of money. So in the-- like in the thrift shop, we bought some ski boots from her. So-- and so we had clothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then after that, we were a few weeks with the Salvation Army. Because somehow, our papers didn't come. And we were in the Salvation Army in the home and everything. They tried to convert us. But otherwise they were very nice. I was even sick there, and they called the doctor. And they treated me. They were very nice. And in the meantime, I guess my mother had communication with this Mrs. [? Morton's ?] [INAUDIBLE]. And she had come the same route that we came with her two children. She made it. And they told us later that she took sleeping pills and was fast asleep, going into France over the border, and that the children just said, oh, my mom is asleep. And here are the papers. And they let her sleep, and they-- she slipped through like that. And she somehow came into this camp, into Swiss camp, first. And she wrote to us, that Buren-- it was that camp-- that it is so nice, and we even have curtains on the window. And why don't you try to come here and every-- so my mother said, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to the commandant of this little town. And he said, look. She said, we are here for so long already.

speaker: interviewee
text: We would like to go to the camp Buren, to visit-- to be with our friends. And the Commandant said, Mrs. Blachmann, I don't think you want to go to Buren. This is not for you. Stay here. You're better off here. No, we want to go to Buren.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the guard took us again, the police. and I-- and we came there. And it was terrible.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was barracks. And then in the meantime, it was November or so. It was muddy, and cold, and the-- the toilets were, you know, like in the military thing, were across from the barracks. There was nothing in the-- and my mother got a bladder infection. And it was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there, we saw also, the first refugees that had come out, of girls. You know? And we saw in what terrible condition they were already from the two years in the French camp. And so it was very depressing for my mother, you know, knowing that my father had gone through this already. So we were in Buren only-- I don't-- not too long. And in the meantime, Werner, my-- Werner Marcus had come to Switzerland too. And we had a-- we had an address in Zurich.

speaker: interviewee
text: He had an uncle in Zurich. And we had always said, if we ever make it to Switzerland, whoever come-- comes there first should write to this uncle, so we know from each other. So I had written. And when he came to-- he made it through to Zurich, without being caught. He just got, in Geneva, on the train and boarded and traveled right through to Zurich on the Swiss train. Everybody has different experiences. He came by himself. And so when he got there, his uncle said, look, I have a postcard for you. Your friend, Gerda, is in Switzerland, is in Buren.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we knew. We communicated. And by coincidence-- I mean, he went by himself to the police in Zurich. Because the uncle didn't want to keep him illegally, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he presented himself, and said, I'm here. And I'm-- this is my name. And he also had a false identity card.

speaker: interviewer
text: So by coincidence, again, we ended up in-- in camps that were not next to each other, but they were, more or less, in walking distance, not by American standards, but about two hours walking, over hills and over-- but we--

speaker: interviewee
text: This first winter, we couldn't see each other. We weren't allowed out. And I think they weren't allowed out. But then the next summer, we could go, I think, weekends or so. We-- we had furlough. We could go for an afternoon and meet.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was a little town, Liestal, in the middle of it. So we-- we met in Liestal. And we had coffee somewhere, or were sitting somewhere, and we met. And that's where our courtship started again, you know, in the springtime. And everything was very romantic.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was not so romantic. He had frozen ears from being in the hiding in France. And he had frozen [? hand ?] fingers. And his teeth were bad, you know. He was also in [INAUDIBLE] for these two years. But he was young, and, you now, most young people survived much better, and endured much better, and were more optimistic, or more energetic. And so we started our friendship. And then one day, I-- we were in that camp, in-- in Bienenberg, it was called. It's still there, I hear. It's a hotel near-- between Basel and Liestal, up on the hill [? light. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this still camp Buren that you're talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was Bienenberg. We got out of Buren and got to this-- this camp. That was an old hotel. And we were housed in-- in regular rooms, mostly three in a room, too. My mother and I. And there was another young lady was with us in this room. And that was already better. We were out of the weather. And we-- we had the bathroom on the floor, at least. And we had a dining room, and the food wasn't great. But we were never hungry, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you treated?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to work while you were either of these camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they made-- the men had, really, harder work. But in his camp, in the men's camp. But we were mostly doing make work, you know? They had-- they cut strips of fabric. And were weaving those rags that you-- they still do. And-- and of course, they had to work in the kitchen. Some worked in the kitchen. Some working in the cleaning. And some worked in the laundry. You know. But I had already started my apprenticeship as-- as a dressmaker. So there was a dressmaker, and from a Viennese Viennese dressmaker in this camp, also. And there came boxes of donated clothing that people had donated to the refugees. And they had to be altered because they didn't always fit. So I told her I can sew, and I can do a little bit. So I worked with her. And I learned how to do alterations and how to-- you know, I learned that later on, they had-- they emptied a big room. And they had babies in there. They have refugee babies that are there. But they didn't want each mother to take care of its baby. They wanted to streamline that. So there was a trained baby nurse from France. And she requested that I help with her. So I did this, you know. But people, they were occupied. Everybody had some kind of an occupation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were paid for your work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Well, very minimum, maybe. But then we had-- later, we had every six weeks, the railroad gave us a 100-mile radius, free transportation. And every six weeks, we could go for two or three days on-- on furlough. Because the families were mostly separated, the husbands and wives. The men were in one camp, and the wives were in the other. So in order to facilitate this-- well, I didn't have a husband. And neither did my mother. But I went with Werner a few times. We were in Basel, and we were in-- in-- in the winter we went to Basel. And in the summer, we went on lakes. And so our friendship grew, and our courtship grew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then one day, the-- the director of my camp came and said, they have a-- an enquiry for dressmaker. For operator, we would call it here, in the blouse factory in Bern, and would I want to go to Bern and work there on a power machine?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, yeah. So when Werner heard that, he put in the only way he could get out of the camp was being a dishwasher. So he-- they were short on those labor-intensive things.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he put in for dishwasher, and within one week, he was in Bern, washing dishes. And I was still in the camp because my liberation took a little longer.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we finally got together. And he had to live on top in the-- in the attic with other dishwashers, other crooks. And it was very primitive, and very amazing to us how unsanitary and primitive the conditions were in a first-class-- so-called first-class restaurant in Bern. But he made it through. And I had that job in the blouse factory. And then we decided to get married. And through different circumstances, too. So we-- my mother gave her consent. And on the 1st of May in 1945, we got married in Bern. And in the same newspaper that our, you know, the little notice appeared that we got married. The headline of that newspaper was, 'Hitler is Dead and the War is Almost Over.' And that was a very memorable day. So I still have that newspaper too. And we got married. And, you know, we-- we lived in Bern then. And my mother was free in Bern too. She wanted to be near me, so she took a job as a maid in a Swiss household. And so we lived in Bern for awhile.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
